findings.title: Reflected XSS in OAuth Callback Error Page
findings.issue: The OAuth callback handler writes error messages from query parameters into HTML after HTML-escaping and sanitizing control characters. The fix (html.EscapeString + sanitizeOAuthParam) is present. This finding is resolved.
findings.impact: Previously exploitable for phishing via localhost callback. Now mitigated.
findings.recommendation: No action required — fix is in place at line 60 using html.EscapeString.

findings.title: EFS Security Group Allows All Egress
findings.issue: The EFS security group allows all outbound traffic to `0.0.0.0/0` on all ports and protocols. EFS mount targets only need inbound NFS (port 2049) and do not initiate outbound connections.
findings.impact: Minimal direct risk, but defense-in-depth suggests removing unnecessary egress from the EFS security group.
findings.recommendation:
Remove the egress rule from the EFS security group entirely, or scope it to only the VPC CIDR if required:

```hcl
resource "aws_security_group" "efs" {
  # Remove the egress block entirely for EFS mount targets
  ...
}
```

findings.title: Example Script Uses assignPublicIp=ENABLED
findings.issue: The example launch script uses `assignPublicIp=ENABLED` when launching Fargate tasks. The production Lambda handler correctly uses `DISABLED`.
findings.impact: If an operator copies this example script for production use, Fargate tasks would receive public IP addresses, exposing the SSM agent and container services to the internet. The example is in `examples/` and is not used by automation, but it sets a risky precedent.
findings.recommendation: Change to `assignPublicIp=DISABLED` in the example script, or add a warning comment explaining the security implications.

findings.title: oc_version Not Validated in Lambda Handler
findings.issue: The `oc_version` parameter from the request body is accepted without validation and passed directly into the task definition environment variables and task tags. While `validate_identifier()` is called for `investigation_id` and `cluster_id`, `oc_version` is not validated against the set of valid versions (4.14-4.20).
findings.impact: A malicious or errant client could inject arbitrary strings into the `OC_VERSION` environment variable and task tags. The entrypoint script does validate the value (`if [ -x "/opt/openshift/${OC_VERSION}/oc" ]`) so invalid versions would fall back to default, but the unvalidated tag value could pollute tagging/auditing.

findings.title: Terraform AWS Provider Broadly Pinned and Lock File Gitignored
findings.issue: The AWS provider is pinned with `version = "~> 5.0"`, which allows any 5.x release. The `.terraform.lock.hcl` file is explicitly gitignored in both the root `.gitignore` and `deploy/regional/.gitignore`, meaning different Terraform runs on different machines could use different provider versions without detection.
findings.impact: A compromised or buggy provider release within the 5.x range could be automatically used in CI or by operators. Without a committed lock file, there is no mechanism to detect provider version drift between runs.
findings.recommendation:
Pin to a more specific range (e.g., `"~> 5.80"`) and remove `.terraform.lock.hcl` from `.gitignore`, then commit it:

```
# In .gitignore: remove the .terraform.lock.hcl line
git add deploy/regional/.terraform.lock.hcl
git commit -m 'chore: commit Terraform provider lock file'
```

findings.title: Unpinned Fedora Base Image
findings.issue: The base image `FROM fedora:43` is pinned to the major version but not to a specific digest. While `fedora:43` is better than `fedora:latest`, the tag can be overwritten when new builds of Fedora 43 are published.
findings.impact: Rebuilds of the container could silently pull a different base image with different packages or configurations. For a security-sensitive SRE tooling container, reproducible builds are important.
findings.recommendation:
Pin to a digest:

```dockerfile
FROM fedora:43@sha256:<digest>
```

findings.title: Claude Code Installed via Piped Shell Script Without Integrity Check
findings.issue: Claude Code is installed by piping a remote script to bash: `curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | INSTALL_DIR=/usr/local/lib/claude-code BIN_DIR=/usr/local/bin bash`. This pattern is vulnerable to man-in-the-middle or compromised CDN attacks at build time. There is no checksum or signature verification.
findings.impact: A compromised install script could inject malicious code into the container image. The `-fsSL` flags ensure the download is from HTTPS, reducing MITM risk, but there is no integrity verification (checksum, signature).
findings.recommendation: If Claude Code publishes checksums or release signatures, verify them after download. Alternatively, download the installer in a separate step and verify its hash before executing.

findings.title: GitHub Actions Pinned to Mutable Version Tags, Not SHAs
findings.issue: All GitHub Actions in both CI workflows are pinned to mutable version tags (e.g., `actions/checkout@v4`, `codecov/codecov-action@v4`, `EnricoMi/publish-unit-test-result-action/composite@v2`) rather than immutable commit SHAs. A malicious actor who compromises these action repositories could push a new tag pointing to malicious code.
findings.impact: For a FedRAMP system, supply chain integrity of CI/CD tooling is important. A compromised action with repository-level write permissions could exfiltrate secrets, modify deployment artifacts, or tamper with test results. The `localstack-tests` workflow has access to `LOCALSTACK_AUTH_TOKEN`.
findings.recommendation:
Pin all actions to their SHA:

```yaml
uses: actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683  # v4.2.2
uses: actions/setup-python@0b93645e9fea7318ecaed2b359559ac225c90a2f  # v5.3.0
uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3  # pin to SHA
```

Use `renovate` or `dependabot` with `pin-digests: true` to automate SHA pinning.

findings.title: GitHub Actions Pinned to Mutable Version Tags, Not SHAs
findings.issue: All GitHub Actions in both CI workflows are pinned to mutable version tags (e.g., `actions/checkout@v4`, `codecov/codecov-action@v4`, `EnricoMi/publish-unit-test-result-action/composite@v2`) rather than immutable commit SHAs.
findings.impact: A compromised action with repository-level write permissions could exfiltrate secrets, modify deployment artifacts, or tamper with security findings before SARIF upload.
findings.recommendation: Pin all actions to their SHA. Use renovate with `pin-digests: true` to automate SHA pinning.

findings.title: uv Install Script in CI Piped Without Integrity Verification
findings.issue: Both `localstack-tests` and `lambda-unit-tests` jobs install `uv` by piping a remote script to `sh`: `curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh`. While HTTPS is used, there is no hash verification of the installer itself.
findings.impact: A compromised astral.sh CDN could serve a malicious installer to CI, potentially affecting Lambda build reproducibility or test validity in a FedRAMP pipeline.
findings.recommendation:
Use the official `astral-sh/setup-uv` GitHub Action (pinned to a SHA) instead:

```yaml
- uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@...<SHA>...
  with:
    version: '0.4.x'
```

This avoids the curl-pipe pattern and leverages GitHub's action verification.

findings.title: Default log_retention_days of 7 Days Is Insufficient for FedRAMP
findings.issue: The `log_retention_days` variable defaults to 7 days. This applies to all Lambda CloudWatch log groups (`/aws/lambda/...`) and the ECS container log group (`/ecs/...`). FedRAMP (NIST SP 800-53 AU-11) requires audit records to be retained for at least 90 days online with 1 year offline, and the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark requires CloudWatch log groups to have a retention period of at least 365 days.
findings.impact: Operational logs for Lambda invocations (including OIDC validation errors, task creation events, and ECS exec sessions) would be automatically deleted after 7 days. An incident discovered after 7 days would have no supporting log evidence. This directly violates FedRAMP AU-11 audit record retention requirements.
findings.recommendation:
Increase the default to meet FedRAMP minimums:

```hcl
variable "log_retention_days" {
  default = 365  # FedRAMP requires 1 year minimum
}
```

Consider separate `active_log_retention_days` (90 days, CloudWatch) and `archive_log_retention_days` (1 year, S3 export) to manage cost.

findings.title: Lambda ECS IAM Policy Uses Wildcard Resource
findings.issue: The Lambda IAM policy grants `ecs:RunTask`, `ecs:StopTask`, `ecs:ListTasks`, `ecs:DescribeTasks`, `ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition`, `ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition`, `ecs:DeregisterTaskDefinition`, and `ecs:TagResource` on `Resource = "*"`. This allows the Lambda function to manage tasks and task definitions across any ECS cluster in the account.
findings.impact: If the Lambda is compromised (e.g., via a token validation bypass or dependency supply chain attack), the attacker can launch, stop, or register task definitions in any cluster in the AWS account.
findings.recommendation:
Scope ECS resources to the specific cluster:

```hcl
Resource = [
  aws_ecs_cluster.main.arn,
  "arn:aws:ecs:${data.aws_region.current.name}:${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:task/${aws_ecs_cluster.main.name}/*",
  "arn:aws:ecs:${data.aws_region.current.name}:${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:task-definition/${var.project}-${var.stage}*"
]
```

Note: `RegisterTaskDefinition` and `DeregisterTaskDefinition` must use `*`, but `RunTask`, `StopTask`, and `TagResource` can be scoped.

findings.title: Shared SRE Role Grants KMS Decrypt on All Keys
findings.issue: The `KMSForECSExec` statement in the shared SRE role's policy grants `kms:Decrypt` and `kms:GenerateDataKey` on `Resource = "*"`. This allows any SRE who assumes this role to decrypt data encrypted with any KMS key in the account (subject to key policy).
findings.impact: An SRE with this role could potentially decrypt data outside the scope of ECS Exec sessions if other KMS keys have permissive key policies. The task role (`aws_iam_role_policy.task_kms` at `iam.tf:224`) correctly scopes to `aws_kms_key.exec_session.arn`, making this inconsistency notable.
findings.recommendation:
Scope the KMS resource to the specific exec session key:

```hcl
Resource = aws_kms_key.exec_session.arn
```

findings.title: CloudWatch Logs Encryption Disabled for SSM Sessions
findings.issue: The ECS cluster's execute command configuration sets `cloud_watch_encryption_enabled = false`. The CloudWatch log group for SSM sessions (`ssm_sessions`) also lacks a `kms_key_id` attribute. All Lambda CloudWatch log groups and the ECS container log group also lack CMK encryption.
findings.impact: SSM session logs may contain sensitive commands and output from SRE investigations into production clusters. Without CMK encryption on log groups, there is no KMS key policy gate on who can read these audit logs. AWS account-level admins and anyone with CloudWatch Logs `GetLogEvents` can read the logs without a separate KMS authorization check.
findings.recommendation:
Enable CloudWatch encryption for ECS Exec and attach the KMS key to all sensitive log groups:

```hcl
cloud_watch_encryption_enabled = true
```

Add `kms_key_id = aws_kms_key.exec_session.arn` to the `ssm_sessions`, `rosa_boundary`, and Lambda log groups.

findings.title: S3 Audit Bucket Uses SSE-S3 Instead of SSE-KMS
findings.issue: The S3 audit bucket uses `sse_algorithm = "AES256"` (SSE-S3) instead of `aws:kms` (SSE-KMS). SSE-S3 uses AWS-managed keys with no audit trail for key usage and no ability to control access via KMS key policies.
findings.impact: For an audit log bucket with WORM compliance (Object Lock), the inability to independently audit encryption key usage or enforce key-level access controls weakens the security posture. Any principal with `s3:GetObject` permission can decrypt objects; there is no separate `kms:Decrypt` gate. This violates the FedRAMP SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest) enhanced requirement for separate key management.
findings.recommendation:
Use KMS encryption with a dedicated CMK:

```hcl
apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
  sse_algorithm     = "aws:kms"
  kms_master_key_id = aws_kms_key.audit_bucket.arn
}
```

findings.title: No EFS Filesystem Policy Enforcing IAM Authorization
findings.issue: The EFS filesystem has no `aws_efs_file_system_policy` resource. While individual access points require IAM (`iam = "ENABLED"` in the task definition), the filesystem itself does not enforce IAM authorization or deny anonymous access at the filesystem level. Any entity with network access to the mount targets (port 2049) could potentially mount the filesystem directly without an access point.
findings.impact: If the Fargate security group or VPC network controls are misconfigured, an entity with network access to NFS port 2049 could mount the EFS filesystem as root, bypassing access point isolation and accessing all investigation data across all users.

findings.title: No S3 Bucket Policy Enforcing Encryption in Transit
findings.category: Infrastructure — Missing Access Control and Auditability
findings.issue: The S3 audit bucket has no `aws_s3_bucket_policy` to enforce encryption in transit (deny non-TLS requests). Without a deny-non-TLS bucket policy, objects could theoretically be uploaded or accessed over unencrypted HTTP.
findings.impact: Without a deny-non-TLS policy, S3 SDK misconfigurations or internal tooling could access audit data over plaintext HTTP. FedRAMP SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity) requires encryption in transit, and the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark explicitly requires an S3 bucket policy denying non-secure transport.

findings.title: Lambda CORS Allows All Origins
findings.issue: Both the Lambda function URL CORS configuration (`allow_origins = ["*"]`) and the Lambda handler's response headers (`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`) permit cross-origin requests from any domain. The Terraform comment says "Allow localhost for testing; restrict in production", but this is not stage-gated.
findings.impact: A malicious website could make cross-origin requests to the Lambda function URL. Since the primary auth mechanism is SigV4 (which cannot be replicated cross-origin) and the OIDC token is passed as a custom header, actual exploitation risk is low. However, the wildcard CORS combined with `Access-Control-Allow-Headers: content-type, x-oidc-token` means if an attacker obtains a valid OIDC token, they could invoke the function from any origin.
findings.recommendation: Restrict CORS origins to known SRE tool domains or remove CORS entirely if the function is only called via direct SDK invocation (which is the current architecture per the lambda-invoker role).

findings.title: Lambda CORS Allows All Origins
findings.issue: The Lambda handler's `response()` function returns `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on every response, permitting cross-origin requests from any domain.
findings.impact: See M7 above — both the Terraform CORS configuration and the handler-level CORS header are wildcard. Both must be fixed.
findings.recommendation: Change to `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://your-sre-portal.example.com` or remove the header entirely if the Lambda is only invoked via direct AWS SDK calls.

findings.title: Container Grants Passwordless Sudo ALL to sre User
findings.issue: The SRE user is granted `NOPASSWD: ALL` sudo access: `echo 'sre ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL' > /etc/sudoers.d/sre`. This allows any user who connects via ECS Exec to escalate to root inside the container.
findings.impact: A compromised SRE session or an insider threat can escalate to root inside the container. Root access enables modification of entrypoint/cleanup scripts (bypassing S3 audit sync), reading other users' EFS data if access point isolation is bypassed, installing tools, or attempting lateral movement via the task role's IAM credentials. All container processes share the same task role credentials regardless of the Linux user.
findings.recommendation:
If root access is required for specific operations (e.g., `alternatives --set`), restrict sudo to specific commands:

```
sre ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/sbin/alternatives
```

The entrypoint already runs as root and handles `alternatives` switching before the user connects, so the SRE user may not need sudo at all.

findings.title: Python Lambda Dependencies Use >= Version Pinning in pyproject.toml
findings.issue: Lambda dependencies use `>=` version specifiers in `pyproject.toml`: `PyJWT>=2.8.0`, `cryptography>=41.0.0`, `requests>=2.31.0`, `boto3>=1.34.0`. Although a `uv.lock` file is committed (which pins exact versions for local development), the Lambda Makefile's `deps` target runs `uv export --frozen` which resolves from the lock file. However, the loose pyproject.toml specifiers mean that updating the lockfile (`uv lock --upgrade`) could pull in any newer version, and there is no lock file hash verification in the deployment pipeline.
findings.impact: Supply chain risk: a compromised version of PyJWT, cryptography, or requests could be automatically pulled in when the lock file is updated. The `cryptography` package in particular has been targeted in supply chain attacks. For FedRAMP SA-12 (Supply Chain Protection), the deployment pipeline should use exact, hash-verified dependencies.
findings.recommendation:
Pin exact versions with hashes in pyproject.toml:

```toml
dependencies = [
    "PyJWT==2.8.0",
    "cryptography==42.0.8",
    "requests==2.32.3",
    "boto3==1.34.150",
]
```

Verify uv.lock hash integrity in CI before building the Lambda package.

findings.title: Bedrock IAM Policy Allows All Regions and All Models
findings.issue: The Bedrock policy grants `bedrock:InvokeModel` and `bedrock:InvokeModelWithResponseStream` on `arn:aws:bedrock:*:*:inference-profile/*` and `arn:aws:bedrock:*:*:foundation-model/*`. The wildcard regions and all-model access mean the task role can invoke any foundation model in any region.
findings.impact: If an SRE's container is compromised, the attacker can invoke arbitrary Bedrock models (potentially expensive ones) in any region, leading to cost abuse. Additionally, data sent to Bedrock models outside the expected region may violate data residency requirements — a critical concern for FedRAMP where data must remain in authorized US regions.
findings.recommendation:
Scope to the specific region and model(s) needed:

```hcl
Resource = [
  "arn:aws:bedrock:${data.aws_region.current.name}::foundation-model/anthropic.claude-*",
  "arn:aws:bedrock:${data.aws_region.current.name}:${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:inference-profile/*"
]
```

findings.title: ECS Task and Lambda IAM Roles Lack Confused Deputy Protection
findings.issue:
The ECS task role, ECS execution role, and Lambda execution roles all use bare service principal trust policies without `aws:SourceAccount` or `aws:SourceArn` conditions:

```json
{"Principal": {"Service": "ecs-tasks.amazonaws.com"}, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"}
```

This pattern is vulnerable to the confused deputy problem: any ECS task in the same account (or cross-account if SCP allows) could potentially assume these roles if the service principal is not conditioned.
findings.impact: While ECS role assumption is constrained by task definition ARN in practice, lacking `aws:SourceArn` or `aws:SourceAccount` conditions means the roles are technically assumable by any ECS task in the account that can register an appropriate task definition. For Lambda roles, this means any Lambda in the account (with IAM permission to assume) could assume the execution role. The missing condition weakens the blast radius isolation between different ECS workloads sharing an account.

findings.title: ECS Task and Lambda IAM Roles Lack Confused Deputy Protection
findings.issue: The create-investigation Lambda execution role trust policy lacks `aws:SourceAccount` or `aws:SourceArn` conditions on the `lambda.amazonaws.com` principal.
findings.impact: See M11 above — any Lambda function in the account could attempt to assume this role if granted `sts:AssumeRole` by some other policy.
findings.recommendation: Add `aws:SourceAccount` condition to Lambda trust policies.

findings.title: Lambda Missing elasticfilesystem:DeleteAccessPoint Permission for Error Cleanup
findings.category: Application — Missing IAM Permission Causing Silent Failures
findings.issue: The Lambda's EFS IAM policy (`efs-access-point-management`) grants only `CreateAccessPoint`, `DescribeAccessPoints`, and `TagResource`. However, the Lambda handler calls `efs.delete_access_point()` in four error-cleanup paths (lines 671, 727, 756, 782 in handler.py). These calls will silently fail with `AccessDeniedException` because the permission is not granted.
findings.impact: When task registration or task launch fails after an EFS access point was newly created, the cleanup code cannot delete the orphaned access point. This leads to EFS access point accumulation. The EFS access point limit is 10,000 per filesystem. In a sustained attack or failure storm, the limit could be reached, preventing new investigations from being created. Additionally, orphaned access points (with their embedded `cluster_id`/`investigation_id` tags and OIDC `sub` values) linger without audit trail.
findings.recommendation:
Add `elasticfilesystem:DeleteAccessPoint` to the Lambda EFS policy, scoped to the filesystem ARN:

```hcl
{
  Effect = "Allow"
  Action = [
    "elasticfilesystem:CreateAccessPoint",
    "elasticfilesystem:DescribeAccessPoints",
    "elasticfilesystem:DeleteAccessPoint",
    "elasticfilesystem:TagResource"
  ]
  Resource = aws_efs_file_system.sre_home.arn
}
```

findings.title: Lambda Missing elasticfilesystem:DeleteAccessPoint Permission for Error Cleanup
findings.category: Application — Missing IAM Permission Causing Silent Failures
findings.issue: The handler calls `efs.delete_access_point()` in four error-cleanup paths but the Lambda execution role lacks `elasticfilesystem:DeleteAccessPoint` permission. The calls are silently swallowed by bare `except Exception: pass` blocks.
findings.impact: Orphaned EFS access points accumulate silently on every failed task launch. See M12 above for full impact.
findings.recommendation: Grant `elasticfilesystem:DeleteAccessPoint` in the Lambda IAM policy. Also replace bare `except Exception: pass` with logging so failures are visible in CloudWatch.

findings.title: ECS Task Role Grants s3:PutObjectAcl on Audit Bucket
findings.issue: The ECS task role's S3 policy grants `s3:PutObjectAcl` on the audit bucket. The audit bucket has `block_public_acls = true` and `ignore_public_acls = true` which prevents making objects public, but `PutObjectAcl` is still an unnecessary permission that violates least privilege. With Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode, ACLs cannot be used to bypass retention, but the permission is still wider than needed.
findings.impact: An SRE container with the task role could set object ACLs on audit log objects (e.g., grant read access to another canonical user). While public ACLs are blocked, cross-account ACL grants are not prevented by the public access block settings. This could allow an insider to make audit evidence readable by an attacker-controlled AWS account.
findings.recommendation: Remove `s3:PutObjectAcl` from the task role policy. The audit sync only needs `s3:PutObject`, `s3:GetObject`, and `s3:ListBucket`.

findings.title: EFS Not Encrypted with Customer-Managed KMS Key
findings.issue: The EFS filesystem uses `encrypted = true` but does not specify a `kms_key_id`. Without an explicit key, AWS uses the aws/elasticfilesystem AWS-managed key, which does not allow custom key policies, cross-account access control, or independent key usage audit via CloudTrail's KMS data events.
findings.impact: For FedRAMP SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest), using an AWS-managed key rather than a CMK means there is no way to restrict which IAM principals can access the encryption key, no ability to rotate the key on demand, and no separate CloudTrail audit of key usage. Investigation data (SRE home directories) could be decrypted by any principal with `elasticfilesystem:*` access without a separate KMS gate.
findings.recommendation:
Create a dedicated KMS CMK for EFS encryption:

```hcl
resource "aws_efs_file_system" "sre_home" {
  encrypted  = true
  kms_key_id = aws_kms_key.efs_sre_home.arn
  ...
}
```

findings.title: No CloudTrail or GuardDuty Configuration in Terraform Scope
findings.issue: The Terraform deployment contains no `aws_cloudtrail` resource, no `aws_guardduty_detector`, and no `aws_config_*` resources. While these may be deployed at the organization/account level separately, there is no validation or dependency ensuring they are enabled for this workload's region. There are no S3 data event trails for the audit bucket, no CloudTrail insights for API anomaly detection, and no explicit GuardDuty threat detection.
findings.impact: For a FedRAMP system, missing CloudTrail coverage means API calls to ECS, EFS, Lambda, KMS, and IAM are not audited. FedRAMP controls AU-2 (Event Logging), AU-3 (Content of Audit Records), and AU-12 (Audit Record Generation) require that all security-relevant events are captured. Without CloudTrail data events on the S3 audit bucket, access to WORM audit evidence is not itself audited — creating an audit log that cannot itself be audited.

findings.title: Lambda Function Not Deployed Inside VPC — JWKS Fetching Over Public Internet
findings.issue: The create-investigation Lambda has no `vpc_config` block, meaning it runs outside the VPC and fetches JWKS public keys from the Keycloak endpoint over the public internet. For a FedRAMP system where Keycloak may be hosted on OpenShift/ROSA, the Lambda must egress to a public endpoint to validate tokens. There are no VPC endpoints defined in the Terraform for Lambda, ECS, EFS, or Secrets Manager, suggesting all AWS API calls from the Lambda also go over the public internet.
findings.impact: Token validation network path is over the public internet without VPC-level traffic inspection or control. If Keycloak is or becomes internet-facing, JWKS keys are fetched without intermediate network security controls. AWS API calls from the Lambda (ECS, EFS, STS) egress over the public internet rather than through VPC endpoints, potentially bypassing VPC-level SCPs or network policies. This violates FedRAMP SC-7 (Boundary Protection) requirements for controlled network boundaries.
findings.recommendation:
Deploy the Lambda inside the VPC with a VPC config:

```hcl
vpc_config {
  subnet_ids         = var.subnet_ids
  security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.lambda.id]
}
```

Add VPC Interface Endpoints for ECS, EFS, STS, SecretsManager, and Lambda to keep traffic private. Add a security group for the Lambda that allows only necessary egress (Keycloak HTTPS, AWS service endpoints).

findings.title: ABAC Tag Bypass: ExecuteCommandOnOwnedTasks Uses Resource Wildcard
findings.issue:
The `ExecuteCommandOnOwnedTasks` statement grants `ecs:ExecuteCommand` on `Resource = "*"` with a StringEquals condition on `ecs:ResourceTag/<abac_tag_key>`. The `abac_tag_key` is configurable (default: `username`, or `uuid` for Red Hat EmployeeIDP). The StringEquals condition uses `$${aws:PrincipalTag/<key>}` which resolves at policy evaluation time from the STS session tag set during `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`.

However, the `DescribeAndListECS` statement also grants `ecs:DescribeTasks`, `ecs:ListTasks`, and `ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition` on `Resource = "*"` with no conditions. This means any authenticated SRE can enumerate ALL tasks and task definitions in the cluster, including those belonging to other users.
findings.impact: While an SRE cannot exec into another user's task (the ABAC condition prevents this), they can: (1) list all running tasks in the cluster including other users' investigation IDs, cluster IDs, and OIDC subjects from task tags; (2) describe all task definitions including investigation metadata baked into environment variables; (3) derive another user's active investigation state from tag data. This is an information disclosure vulnerability — the investigation isolation model leaks who is investigating what cluster, when, and with what timeout.
findings.recommendation:
Scope `DescribeAndListECS` to the authenticated user's own tasks using ABAC, or at minimum filter by cluster ARN:

```hcl
{
  Sid    = "DescribeAndListECS"
  Effect = "Allow"
  Action = ["ecs:DescribeTasks", "ecs:ListTasks", "ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition"]
  Resource = aws_ecs_cluster.main.arn
}
```

For task-level describe, add a separate statement with the ABAC condition matching task tag to principal tag.

findings.title: Root Sudo ALL Enables Entrypoint SIGKILL to Bypass S3 Audit Sync
findings.issue: The SRE user has `NOPASSWD: ALL` sudo (Containerfile line 66). The entrypoint runs as root and is the container's PID 1. S3 audit sync runs only in `cleanup()` (SIGTERM/SIGINT/SIGHUP trap) and at normal exit. SIGKILL cannot be trapped. An SRE who connects via ECS Exec can execute `sudo kill -9 1` to send SIGKILL to the entrypoint process (PID 1), killing it instantly without triggering any signal handler. The container terminates and the S3 sync of /home/sre never runs.
findings.impact: A malicious SRE can suppress the S3 audit evidence upload by killing the container entrypoint with SIGKILL before ECS stops the task. All investigation artifacts stored in /home/sre (commands run, downloaded data, must-gather output, notes) are permanently lost — not uploaded to the WORM audit bucket. This directly compromises the ZOA audit integrity guarantee. Combines with M8 (NOPASSWD: ALL sudo) and the architectural limitation that SIGKILL cannot be caught by bash signal traps.
findings.recommendation: Remove `NOPASSWD: ALL` sudo (see M8) — restrict to specific binaries such as `/usr/sbin/alternatives` only. With no root access, the SRE cannot kill PID 1. Additionally, consider an ECS task lifecycle hook or sidecar container that syncs /home/sre continuously (e.g., via `inotifywait` or a periodic `aws s3 sync`) rather than only on exit, so evidence is preserved even on abnormal termination.

findings.title: Root Sudo ALL Enables Entrypoint SIGKILL to Bypass S3 Audit Sync
findings.issue: The signal trap at line 46 covers SIGTERM, SIGINT, and SIGHUP but cannot catch SIGKILL. The S3 sync at line 143 executes at normal exit but not if the entrypoint is killed with signal 9. Since the entrypoint runs as PID 1 and the SRE has unrestricted sudo (M8), the SRE can `sudo kill -9 1` to terminate the process without triggering any cleanup.
findings.impact: See H4 (Containerfile) — the S3 audit sync is the last line of ZOA audit integrity defense inside the container and can be bypassed by SIGKILL.
findings.recommendation: Restrict SRE sudo to specific commands (see M8) to prevent killing PID 1. Implement a sidecar container or continuous S3 sync so evidence preservation does not depend solely on the entrypoint trap.

findings.title: Authenticated SREs Can Request task_timeout=0 to Disable Reaper Enforcement
findings.issue: The Lambda handler accepts `task_timeout` from the client request body and validates only that it is an integer 0-86400 (line 141-147). Any authenticated SRE can set `task_timeout=0` when calling `start-task`. When `task_timeout=0`, no `deadline` tag is added to the ECS task (handler.py line 694: `if task_timeout > 0:`). The reaper Lambda skips tasks with no `deadline` tag (reap-tasks/handler.py line 99: `if not deadline_str: skipped += 1; continue`). Additionally, the reaper's IAM `ecs:StopTask` permission has a condition `ForAnyValue:StringLike: {ecs:ResourceTag/deadline: "*"}` (lambda-reap-tasks.tf line 69-72), meaning even a manual reaper invocation cannot stop tasks without the tag.
findings.impact: An authenticated SRE can bypass the tamper-proof timeout mechanism by requesting `task_timeout=0` at investigation creation time. The resulting task runs indefinitely — no reaper enforcement, no deadline tag, and the reaper IAM policy actively prevents the reaper from stopping it. This violates the ZOA requirement for bounded session duration. The task can persist until manually stopped by an operator with ECS console/CLI access. The CLAUDE.md states TASK_TIMEOUT is 'not modifiable from container' — this is correct, but it IS freely selectable at task creation by any authenticated SRE.
findings.recommendation:
Enforce a minimum task_timeout in the Lambda handler: reject `task_timeout=0` or set a minimum floor (e.g., 300 seconds). Alternatively, make `task_timeout` an operator-only setting controlled by the Terraform variable alone and remove it from the client request body entirely. The Terraform variable `task_timeout_default` exists for this purpose:

```python
# Remove task_timeout from body parsing; use Lambda env var only:
task_timeout = int(os.environ.get('TASK_TIMEOUT_DEFAULT', '3600'))
```

findings.title: Authenticated SREs Can Request task_timeout=0 to Disable Reaper Enforcement
findings.issue: The reaper IAM policy's `ecs:StopTask` permission requires `ForAnyValue:StringLike: {ecs:ResourceTag/deadline: "*"}`. This prevents the reaper from stopping any task without a `deadline` tag — including tasks where the SRE intentionally set `task_timeout=0`. The combination means tasks with no deadline tag are permanently unmanageable by the reaper.
findings.impact: See H5 (handler.py). The IAM condition locks in the bypass: even a manual reaper Lambda invocation cannot reap a deadline-less task. An operator must use the ECS console or CLI directly.
findings.recommendation: Remove the `ForAnyValue:StringLike` condition from the `ecs:StopTask` IAM statement. Deadline enforcement should be a handler-level decision, not an IAM gate. The current condition was intended as a safety guard but inadvertently creates an unmanageable task class.

findings.title: No OIDC Token Replay Protection: jti Claim Not Tracked
findings.issue: The `_validate_with_jwks()` function validates the JWT signature, `exp`, and `aud` claims, but does not check the `jti` (JWT ID) claim and maintains no token replay store. PyJWT's `decode()` options at lines 316-325 do not include `jti` validation. A valid OIDC token intercepted from network traffic, the CLI token cache (`~/.cache/rosa-boundary/token-cache`), Lambda CloudWatch logs (before redaction was added), or a compromised workstation can be replayed to the Lambda within the token's `exp` window to create additional unauthorized investigations.
findings.impact: A stolen OIDC token can be replayed for its full validity period (typically 5 minutes for Keycloak ID tokens by default, but configurable up to hours) to: (1) create investigations under the victim SRE's identity; (2) assume the lambda-invoker role via STS `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`; (3) assume the shared SRE role and exec into the victim's existing task. The absence of jti tracking means any token theft within its validity window grants full impersonation. For a FedRAMP system where all actions must be attributed to a specific individual, this is a significant audit and access control gap.
findings.recommendation:
Implement jti-based replay protection using DynamoDB or ElastiCache as a short-lived token store:

```python
import boto3, time
dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
table = dynamodb.Table(os.environ['TOKEN_REPLAY_TABLE'])

def check_and_record_jti(claims):
    jti = claims.get('jti')
    if not jti:
        return  # Tokens without jti are not replayable
    exp = claims.get('exp', int(time.time()) + 300)
    try:
        table.put_item(
            Item={'jti': jti, 'exp': exp},
            ConditionExpression='attribute_not_exists(jti)'
        )
    except table.meta.client.exceptions.ConditionalCheckFailedException:
        raise ValueError('Token already used (replay detected)')
```
Alternatively, configure Keycloak to issue short-lived tokens (60-second `exp`) and enforce STS role session duration constraints.

findings.title: KMS Key Deletion Window Is 7 Days — Minimum Allowed, Insufficient for FedRAMP
findings.issue: The `exec_session` KMS key has `deletion_window_in_days = 7`, which is the minimum AWS allows. This key protects ECS Exec session logs (SSM channel encryption) and CloudWatch log groups (when M3 is remediated). A scheduled key deletion, whether accidental or malicious, takes effect in 7 days.
findings.impact: If the KMS key is accidentally scheduled for deletion (or an operator with `kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion` does so), all ECS Exec session logs and CloudWatch log data encrypted with this key become permanently unreadable after 7 days. For FedRAMP systems, audit evidence must be retained for defined periods (AU-11: 90 days online, 1 year offline). Losing the key renders the WORM-protected audit evidence inaccessible despite the S3 Object Lock. This also affects the investigation log integrity required by the ZOA model. 7 days is insufficient detection/response time for a FedRAMP incident response process.
findings.recommendation:
Increase the deletion window to 30 days (the AWS maximum for pending deletion) to give the security team time to detect and cancel accidental key deletion:

```hcl
resource "aws_kms_key" "exec_session" {
  deletion_window_in_days = 30
  ...
}
```

Also add a CloudWatch alarm on the `kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion` CloudTrail event for this key ARN.

findings.title: task_timeout Accepted from Client Request Body Without Operator Maximum Enforcement
findings.issue: The Lambda handler accepts `task_timeout` from the client-supplied request body and validates only that it is an integer 0-86400. The operator configures a default via `TASK_TIMEOUT_DEFAULT` (Terraform `task_timeout_default`, default 3600), but any authenticated SRE can override this to any value up to 24 hours, including the maximum of 86400 seconds (24 hours).
findings.impact: A legitimate SRE can request a 24-hour session even if the operator intended to enforce a 1-hour maximum. While this is not a critical bypass (the SRE is authenticated), it undermines operator policy and reduces the effectiveness of the tamper-proof timeout as a ZOA control. Combined with H5, if `task_timeout=0`, the timeout control is eliminated entirely.
findings.recommendation:
Add a configurable maximum task timeout that operators can set via a separate Lambda environment variable, and enforce it server-side:

```python
MAX_TASK_TIMEOUT = int(os.environ.get('MAX_TASK_TIMEOUT', '86400'))
# ...
if task_timeout > MAX_TASK_TIMEOUT:
    return response(400, {'error': f'task_timeout exceeds maximum allowed value of {MAX_TASK_TIMEOUT} seconds'})
```

This allows operators to set a deployment-specific ceiling independently of the default.

findings.title: stop-task and close-investigation Bypass ABAC Role — Any SRE Can Stop Any Task
findings.issue: The `stop-task` and `close-investigation` CLI commands load ambient AWS credentials via `config.LoadDefaultConfig()` and call `ecs:StopTask` directly, bypassing the ABAC-gated shared SRE role entirely. The shared SRE role's `ExecuteCommandOnOwnedTasks` ABAC condition (line 186-199 of oidc.tf) only covers `ecs:ExecuteCommand`. Neither the SRE role nor the task role grants `ecs:StopTask`. When these commands are used with credentials obtained outside the ABAC flow (e.g., directly configured AWS credentials), the ABAC ownership check is never evaluated.
findings.impact: Any SRE with ambient `ecs:StopTask` permission in the account can stop any other user's task, bypassing the ABAC isolation model that protects task ownership. When combined with `close-investigation`, they can also delete another user's EFS access point — destroying the investigation data and any audit trail not yet uploaded to S3. This directly contradicts the investigation isolation model documented in CLAUDE.md.

findings.title: stop-task and close-investigation Bypass ABAC Role — Any SRE Can Stop Any Task
findings.issue: The `close-investigation` command loads ambient AWS credentials and calls `ecs:StopTask` and `elasticfilesystem:DeleteAccessPoint` without assuming the shared SRE role. There is no ownership check: the EFS access point is found by `ClusterID` + `InvestigationID` tags (which any authenticated SRE can supply), and then deleted without verifying it belongs to the caller.
findings.impact: An SRE knowing another user's `cluster_id` and `investigation_id` (visible via `list-tasks` or the H3 information disclosure finding) can destroy the victim's EFS access point and stop their task. The EFS data persists on the filesystem but the access point deletion is irreversible, permanently blocking the victim's access to their investigation home directory.
findings.recommendation: Add ownership verification before deletion: check that the access point's `username` or `oidc_sub` tag matches the caller's identity. Require OIDC authentication and role assumption matching the ABAC model for close and stop operations.

findings.title: PyJWKClient Instantiated Per Lambda Invocation — No JWKS Caching Between Calls
findings.issue: `PyJWKClient` is instantiated inside `_validate_with_jwks()` on every Lambda invocation (line 313: `jwks_client = PyJWKClient(jwks_url)`). Although `PyJWKClient` has a built-in JWK Set cache (`cache_jwk_set=True`, 300-second lifespan by default), this cache is attached to the client instance, not to a module-level singleton. Since a new client object is created on each call, the JWKS cache is never reused between Lambda invocations. Every call to the Lambda results in an HTTP request to the Keycloak JWKS endpoint (`/protocol/openid-connect/certs`).
findings.impact: Under sustained load, the Lambda will saturate the Keycloak JWKS endpoint with HTTP requests — one per Lambda invocation, up to the Lambda concurrency limit. A concurrency spike (e.g., from a CI system launching many investigations simultaneously) can overwhelm Keycloak, causing JWKS fetch failures. When JWKS fetching fails, `_validate_with_jwks()` returns `None`, causing all token validations to fail and blocking all SRE investigation creation. This is effectively a self-inflicted denial of service against the authentication system. Additionally, each JWKS fetch adds 10-200ms of latency per invocation.
findings.recommendation:
Move `PyJWKClient` instantiation to module level so the instance is reused across warm Lambda invocations, leveraging the built-in JWK Set cache:

```python
# Module-level clients (reused across warm invocations)
_primary_jwks_client = None
_stage_jwks_client = None
_prod_jwks_client = None

def _get_jwks_client(jwks_url: str) -> PyJWKClient:
    # Or use a dict keyed by URL
    return PyJWKClient(jwks_url, cache_jwk_set=True, lifespan=300)
```
Or use a module-level dict keyed by JWKS URL. This ensures the JWKS response is cached for 300 seconds across all warm invocations of the same Lambda container.

findings.title: preferred_username Fallback as ABAC Tag Value Enables Username Collision Attack
findings.issue: When `ABAC_TAG_KEY=username` (the default) and the OIDC token does not contain a `https://aws.amazon.com/tags` claim with `principal_tags`, the handler falls back to `claims.get('preferred_username', user_email)` as the ABAC tag value (line 204). This fallback path applies to any Keycloak deployment that has not configured the AWS session tags protocol mapper. The resulting task is tagged with `username: <preferred_username>`. In many Keycloak deployments, `preferred_username` is user-editable via the self-service account console (`/auth/realms/<realm>/account`). If two SREs have the same `preferred_username`, or if a user changes their username to match another user's, the attacker's session tag `aws:PrincipalTag/username` will match the victim's task tag `ecs:ResourceTag/username`, allowing the ABAC condition to succeed.
findings.impact: A malicious SRE who can change their `preferred_username` in Keycloak to match a target SRE's username can exec into the target's investigation task. This completely breaks the per-user isolation model. The attack is silent — no IAM denial will appear because the ABAC condition genuinely matches. The attacker gains access to the victim's EFS home directory (investigation artifacts, kubeconfig, cluster credentials) and can perform actions in the victim's investigation context.
findings.recommendation:
Do not use `preferred_username` as the ABAC tag value. Instead, use the immutable `sub` claim (OIDC subject UUID) which cannot be changed by the user:

```python
# Use sub as ABAC tag when no principal_tags available
abac_tag_value = user_sub  # immutable, admin-assigned UUID
```
Alternatively, require the AWS session tags mapper to be configured in Keycloak and reject tokens without the `principal_tags` claim when `ABAC_TAG_KEY=username`. Keycloak admins should also disable self-service username changes in the realm settings.

findings.title: PyJWT decode() Does Not Explicitly Verify 'iss' Claim Post-Validation
findings.issue: The `_validate_with_jwks()` function calls `jwt.decode()` with `options` that specify `verify_signature=True`, `verify_exp=True`, and `verify_aud=True`, but does NOT pass an `issuer=` keyword argument and does not include `verify_iss` in options (PyJWT default is to verify `iss` only when `issuer=` is passed). The pre-validation issuer routing in `validate_oidc_token()` checks `token_iss == primary_iss` before dispatching to `_validate_with_jwks()`, which mitigates the most obvious bypass. However, `_validate_with_jwks()` is a generic, reusable function: it accepts `jwks_url` and `client_id` but not an expected `issuer`. If this function is ever called outside `validate_oidc_token()` (e.g., in a test or a future code path), the `iss` claim will not be verified by PyJWT itself.
findings.impact: If the issuer routing check is bypassed or `_validate_with_jwks()` is called directly, a token from any issuer with a valid signature (e.g., a secondary OIDC provider that has been compromised or misconfigured) could be accepted as long as its `aud` and `exp` are valid. For a defense-in-depth security architecture, the signature verifier should independently enforce the expected issuer.
findings.recommendation:
Pass the expected issuer to `jwt.decode()` via the `issuer=` kwarg:

```python
claims = jwt.decode(
    token,
    signing_key.key,
    algorithms=["RS256"],
    audience=client_id,
    issuer=expected_issuer,  # Add this
    options={
        "verify_signature": True,
        "verify_exp": True,
        "verify_aud": True,
        "verify_iss": True   # Add this
    }
)
```
Update `_validate_with_jwks()` to accept an `expected_issuer` parameter.

findings.title: Keycloak Deployed with hostname.strict: false and httpEnabled: true
findings.issue: The Keycloak CR sets `hostname.strict: false` and `http.httpEnabled: true`. Setting `strict: false` disables Keycloak's hostname verification, allowing OIDC tokens to be issued regardless of the `Host` header, which can lead to incorrect `iss` claim values in tokens if Keycloak is accessed via multiple hostnames. Setting `httpEnabled: true` means Keycloak serves HTTP (port 8080) internally; the OpenShift Route terminates TLS at the edge (`termination: edge`), but traffic between the router and the Keycloak pod travels over unencrypted HTTP within the cluster network.
findings.impact: An attacker with network access to the pod network (e.g., via a compromised co-tenant pod in the same namespace or cluster) can perform a man-in-the-middle attack on the HTTP leg between the OpenShift Router and Keycloak. This could allow stealing OIDC tokens mid-flight or injecting malicious JWKS responses. The `hostname.strict: false` setting also means tokens may contain unexpected `iss` values if Keycloak is accessed via internal service DNS, complicating token validation and potentially allowing token confusion.
findings.recommendation:
Enable hostname strictness and require HTTPS end-to-end:

```yaml
spec:
  http:
    httpEnabled: false  # Disable plain HTTP
    httpsEnabled: true  # Enable HTTPS from Keycloak pod
  hostname:
    strict: true  # Enforce hostname validation
    hostname: keycloak.example.com  # Set expected hostname
```
For OpenShift, use `termination: reencrypt` in the Route to re-encrypt traffic to the Keycloak pod:

```yaml
tls:
  termination: reencrypt
  insecureEdgeTerminationPolicy: Redirect
```

findings.title: 409 DuplicateInvestigation Response Leaks Task ARNs to Any Authenticated SRE
findings.issue: When a second authenticated SRE attempts to create an investigation with the same `cluster_id` and `investigation_id` as an existing active investigation, the Lambda returns HTTP 409 with a body containing `existing_tasks` — a list of full ECS task ARNs for the collision (line 268-270). The `find_running_tasks_for_investigation()` function uses the deterministic `startedBy` hash to find tasks, so the collision detection works cross-user. Any authenticated SRE who knows (or guesses) another user's `cluster_id` + `investigation_id` can obtain the victim's task ARN by attempting to create a duplicate investigation.
findings.impact: Task ARNs expose the task ID, cluster name, and region of another user's investigation. A task ID is required to call `ecs:ExecuteCommand`. While the ABAC condition prevents execution into another user's task, leaking task ARNs reduces the complexity of other attacks (H3, H6). Disclosure of another user's active investigation metadata (which cluster they are investigating) violates the investigation isolation model and could be used for operational intelligence by a malicious insider.
findings.recommendation:
Remove `existing_tasks` from the 409 response body. Return only enough information for the caller to know their own investigation is already active:

```python
return response(409, {
    'error': f'Investigation already has a running task',
    'access_point_id': e.access_point_id  # Keep this for the caller's own reference
    # Remove: 'existing_tasks': e.existing_tasks
})
```
Also verify ownership before revealing the access point ID (only return it if the caller's OIDC sub matches the access point's `oidc_sub` tag).

findings.title: login Command Writes OIDC Token to stdout
findings.issue: The `login` subcommand writes the raw OIDC ID token to stdout (`fmt.Fprintln(os.Stdout, token)`). The command's docstring states this is intentional for script integration. However, if an SRE runs `$(rosa-boundary login)` in a shell script or captures output, the full token appears in shell history, process arguments, or terminal scrollback buffers. In environments with terminal session logging (common in FedRAMP-compliant SRE workflows), the token is captured in audit logs in plaintext.
findings.impact: OIDC ID tokens are bearer credentials valid for the full Keycloak token lifetime (often 5-60 minutes). If a token is captured from stdout (shell history, terminal log, CI artifact), an attacker can replay it to: (1) assume the Lambda invoker role via `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`; (2) call the Lambda with the victim's identity; (3) assume the shared SRE role and exec into the victim's tasks (limited by ABAC, but see M18 for bypass). This compounds M15 (no jti replay protection).
findings.recommendation:
Warn users that the token is sensitive and should not be logged:

```go
fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, "Warning: token output below is a bearer credential — do not log or share")
```
Document that the `login` command is intended for programmatic use only; interactive users should use `start-task` directly. Consider whether stdout token output is necessary at all given that the token is cached to disk.

findings.title: Keycloak PostgreSQL Database Deployed Without HA or Backup Configuration
findings.issue: The CloudNativePG cluster for Keycloak is configured with `instances: 1` (single primary, no standbys) and no WAL archival or backup configuration. A PostgreSQL pod failure causes a full Keycloak outage, blocking all SRE authentication and investigation creation. Without WAL archival, Keycloak's realm configuration (OIDC clients, users, groups, mappers) cannot be restored from a backup if the PVC is lost.
findings.impact: A Keycloak database failure blocks all SRE access to the rosa-boundary system: no new investigations can be created, and the OIDC token flow fails. In a production incident scenario, this means SREs are locked out of the investigation tooling precisely when they need it most. Loss of Keycloak database state requires full realm reconfiguration, including re-registering all OIDC clients (including the AWS OIDC provider thumbprint configuration). FedRAMP CP-9 (Information System Backup) and CP-10 (Information System Recovery) require backup and recovery capabilities.
findings.recommendation:
Configure CNPG with at least 2 instances (primary + one standby) and enable WAL archival to S3:

```yaml
spec:
  instances: 2  # Primary + standby for HA
  backup:
    barmanObjectStore:
      destinationPath: s3://your-backup-bucket/keycloak-db/
      s3Credentials:
        accessKeyId:
          name: backup-creds
          key: ACCESS_KEY_ID
        secretAccessKey:
          name: backup-creds
          key: SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
    retentionPolicy: "30d"
```

findings.title: rosa-boundary Container Missing readonlyRootFilesystem Hardening
findings.issue: The `kube-proxy` sidecar container correctly sets `readonlyRootFilesystem = true` (line 176), but the main `rosa-boundary` container definition does not set this property. The root filesystem of the `rosa-boundary` container is writable. While the SRE user has root access via sudo (M8/H4), the writable root filesystem allows modification of system binaries, configuration files (`/etc/sudoers`, `/etc/passwd`), and the entrypoint script itself without needing to remount anything.
findings.impact: A root-capable SRE can modify system files on the writable root filesystem: overwriting `/usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh` (disabling S3 audit sync), modifying `/etc/sudoers.d/sre` (which the current sudoers validation check at Containerfile line 69 cannot prevent at runtime), or replacing CLI binaries. While the EFS mount (`/home/sre`) and the task ephemeral storage are separate, the writable root filesystem provides additional persistence vectors for an insider threat scenario.

findings.title: Group Membership Enforced Only at Application Layer — Any Keycloak User Can Assume SRE Role via Direct STS Call
findings.issue:
The `sre_shared` role trust policy (oidc.tf:88-143) and `lambda_invoker` trust policy (lambda-invoker.tf:13-62) condition only on `<oidc_provider_domain>:aud == oidc_client_id`. They do not enforce group membership (`sre-team`, etc.). Group membership is checked exclusively inside the Lambda handler at handler.py:209-215 — an application-layer control, not an IAM-layer control.

The CLI's start_task.go line 149 calls `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` directly with the original OIDC token and the SRE role ARN — STS validates signature and audience but not Keycloak group membership. The Lambda's group check is not a prerequisite for STS role assumption: any Keycloak user with a valid token for the correct audience can assume `sre_shared` without ever invoking the Lambda.
findings.impact: Any Keycloak-authenticated user (developers, contractors, former employees with non-deprovisioned accounts) who has a valid OIDC token for the `aws-sre-access` audience can call `sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` directly and obtain `sre_shared` credentials. The `DescribeAndListECS` statement (oidc.tf:201-210) has no ABAC conditions, so all running SRE tasks and their metadata (cluster IDs, investigation IDs, OIDC subjects, timeouts, usernames) are enumerable by any Keycloak user. While ABAC prevents exec into others' tasks, the group membership check is purely a soft control.

findings.title: Group Membership Enforced Only at Application Layer — Any Keycloak User Can Assume SRE Role via Direct STS Call
findings.issue: The `lambda_invoker` role trust policy also conditions only on `aud`, not group membership. Any Keycloak-authenticated user can assume this role and invoke the Lambda function directly via `lambda:InvokeFunction`. The Lambda's group check is the only gate between an unauthenticated-to-SRE user and Lambda invocation.
findings.impact: See H7 (oidc.tf). An attacker who assumes `lambda_invoker` and calls the Lambda with a valid OIDC token will be rejected at the group check inside the Lambda handler. However, they can observe the 403 response which discloses group membership details (see L16). The bigger risk is the path to `sre_shared` via direct STS.
findings.recommendation: Add the same group membership condition as described in H7 (oidc.tf) to the `lambda_invoker` trust policy.

findings.title: Group Membership Enforced Only at Application Layer — Any Keycloak User Can Assume SRE Role via Direct STS Call
findings.issue: The CLI calls `awsclient.AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity(cmd.Context(), cfg.AWSRegion, roleARN, idToken, sessionName)` directly at line 149 using the original OIDC token and the SRE role ARN. This is a separate STS API call that does not depend on the Lambda having run or validated group membership. The Lambda returns `role_arn` in its response, but an attacker who already knows the SRE role ARN (from CLI output, documentation, or prior Lambda response) can skip the Lambda entirely.
findings.impact: See H7 (oidc.tf). The STS assumption at line 149 is architecturally decoupled from Lambda group validation.
findings.recommendation: The Lambda should perform the STS role assumption itself and return scoped short-lived credentials (or a session token) rather than returning the role ARN for independent assumption. This makes the Lambda the mandatory authorization checkpoint. Alternatively, add group conditions to the IAM trust policy (see H7 oidc.tf recommendation).

findings.title: OIDC `sub` Claim Not Validated for Presence — None Value Propagates to Audit Tags
findings.issue:
At line 166, `user_sub = claims.get('sub')` returns `None` if the JWT does not contain a `sub` claim (malformed token or misconfigured Keycloak mapper). The `None` value is passed through without validation to `create_investigation_task()` as `oidc_sub=None` (line 230), then to `efs.create_access_point()` Tags at line 625 as `{'Key': 'oidc_sub', 'Value': None}`, and to ECS `run_task` tags at line 683 as `{'key': 'oidc_sub', 'value': None}`. The AWS boto3 APIs will raise a `ValidationException` for `None` tag values, causing an unhandled exception path that returns a generic 500 error.

Beyond the API failure, `oidc_sub` is the immutable audit identifier linking a task to a specific user. A missing sub makes the audit record unattributable. The `sub` claim is mandatory in the OIDC Core spec (RFC 7519) but PyJWT's `jwt.decode()` does not enforce its presence.
findings.impact: A JWT without a `sub` claim will cause a boto3 `ValidationException` during task creation, manifesting as a 500 error with no useful information to the caller. Any audit records referencing the resulting (non-existent) task would have no user attribution. If boto3's behavior changes to coerce `None` to the string `'None'`, audit records would be silently corrupted with a meaningless attribution value.
findings.recommendation:
Add explicit validation of `sub` immediately after token validation returns claims:

```python
user_sub = claims.get('sub')
if not user_sub or not isinstance(user_sub, str) or len(user_sub) == 0:
    logger.warning("Token missing required 'sub' claim")
    return response(401, {'error': 'Invalid token: missing required sub claim'})
```

findings.title: JWT-Derived ABAC Tag Value Has No Length or Character Sanitization
findings.issue: The `abac_tag_value` extracted from JWT `principal_tags` at line 196 (`abac_values[0]`) and the fallback `username` value at line 204 are used directly in ECS tag API calls (lines 684, 625) without length or character validation. AWS STS session tag values and ECS tag values are limited to 256 characters and must match `[\p{L}\p{Z}\p{N}_.:/=+\-@]+`. A Keycloak token mapper misconfiguration or compromise could produce oversized or specially-crafted values. Additionally, if `preferred_username` is `'unknown'` (the default when both `preferred_username` and `email` claims are absent), tasks will be tagged with `username: unknown` — making multiple users' tasks share the same ABAC tag value, breaking per-user isolation.
findings.impact: 1. An oversized or invalid-character ABAC tag value causes a boto3 `ParamValidationError` or `ClientError` during `ecs.tag_resource()` or `ecs.run_task()`, resulting in a 500 error and (when the tag is newly created) an orphaned EFS access point that cannot be cleaned up due to M12. 2. The `username: unknown` fallback case would match another user with the same fallback, allowing ABAC condition match and potential cross-user task exec (depends on M18). 3. A very long `preferred_username` (if unconstrained in Keycloak) would cause API failures on every invocation for that user.
findings.recommendation:
Validate and sanitize `abac_tag_value` and `username` before use:

```python
import re
_TAG_VALUE_RE = re.compile(r'^[\w_.:/=+\-@\s]{1,256}$')

def validate_tag_value(value: str, field: str) -> str:
    if not isinstance(value, str) or not value:
        raise ValueError(f'{field} must be a non-empty string')
    value = value[:256]
    if not _TAG_VALUE_RE.match(value):
        raise ValueError(f'{field} contains invalid characters for an AWS tag')
    return value
```

Also add an explicit check that `abac_tag_value` is never `'unknown'` (the email/username fallback default) before proceeding.

findings.title: CLI Token Cache Uses File Modification Time — JWT `exp` Claim Not Verified Client-Side
findings.issue: `CachedToken()` determines cache validity by comparing file mtime against `cacheValidityPeriod = 4 * time.Minute`. The function never parses the JWT to inspect the actual `exp` claim. Two failure modes result: (1) If Keycloak issues tokens with a lifetime shorter than 4 minutes, the CLI will serve an already-expired token from cache — the Lambda rejects it with 401. (2) If Keycloak issues tokens with a lifetime longer than 4 minutes (typical: 5-60 minutes), a token stolen from the cache file within the 4-minute mtime window remains valid at the Lambda and STS for the full JWT lifetime. The cache eviction policy (4 minutes) does not reflect the actual credential risk window (full `exp` duration).
findings.impact: The real replay window for a stolen cache file token is the JWT `exp` duration (typically 5-60 minutes depending on Keycloak configuration), not 4 minutes. The 4-minute cache only controls re-authentication frequency, not the token's actual validity. This compounds M15 (no jti replay protection): the cached token can be replayed until its `exp` regardless of how long it has been in the cache file.

findings.title: 403 Response Discloses User Group Memberships and Required Group Names
findings.issue: When a user fails the group membership check, the 403 response body contains: (1) `'groups': groups` (line 214) — the complete list of the caller's Keycloak group memberships extracted from the validated JWT; (2) the error message at line 213 includes `{REQUIRED_GROUPS}` — the full list of group names required for access (e.g., `['sre-team', 'platform-sre', 'osd-sre']`). Both pieces of information are returned to the caller in the HTTP response body and would also appear in any access logs or API response captures.
findings.impact: If an attacker obtains a valid OIDC token via replay (M15), stdout capture (L12), or cache theft, and the token belongs to a user not in `sre-team`, the 403 response reveals: (a) the victim's complete Keycloak group membership — useful for privilege mapping and social engineering; (b) the exact group names that grant SRE access — reduces effort for targeting Keycloak group membership manipulation. For a FedRAMP system, group membership is sensitive organizational structure data.
findings.recommendation:
Return only a generic authorization failure message:

```python
return response(403, {
    'error': 'User not authorized: insufficient group membership'
    # Remove 'groups' key and remove REQUIRED_GROUPS from error string
})
```

Log the detailed group information server-side in the Lambda's CloudWatch Logs (already done at line 211) for debugging and audit purposes.

findings.title: AWS Account ID Hardcoded in Committed Keycloak Overlay Configuration
findings.issue: The IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) annotation on the ExternalSecrets service account contains a hardcoded AWS account ID: `arn:aws:iam::641875867446:role/dev-keycloak`. This account ID is also present in `docs/configuration/aws-iam-policies.md` and `deploy/regional/README.md`. While AWS account IDs are not considered secret by AWS, hardcoding them into version-controlled configuration files: (1) permanently links the account ID to the project in git history; (2) makes the account an identifiable target for enumeration; (3) prevents reuse of this overlay across accounts without manual editing.
findings.impact: Account ID disclosure enables targeted enumeration attacks: an attacker knowing the account ID can probe for public S3 buckets, public AMIs, or other inadvertently exposed resources in that specific account. For a FedRAMP system, account IDs should be treated as sensitive configuration that is injected at deployment time, not committed to source control.
findings.recommendation:
Replace the hardcoded account ID with a Kustomize variable or configmap substitution. Use a Kustomize `configMapGenerator` or `vars` section to inject the account ID from a non-committed `.env` file or Kustomize overlay variable:

```yaml
# In kustomization.yaml:
vars:
  - name: AWS_ACCOUNT_ID
    objref:
      kind: ConfigMap
      name: cluster-config
      apiVersion: v1
    fieldref:
      fieldPath: data.awsAccountId
```

Then reference `$(AWS_ACCOUNT_ID)` in the service account annotation. Remove the hardcoded ID from git history using `git filter-repo` or treat the current value as non-sensitive given it is already in the public git history.

findings.title: ECS Task Role s3:GetObject Has No Path Prefix Condition — Cross-User Audit File Read
findings.issue: The ECS task role's S3 policy grants s3:GetObject, s3:ListBucket, s3:PutObject, and s3:PutObjectAcl on the entire audit bucket (aws_s3_bucket.audit.arn and ${aws_s3_bucket.audit.arn}/*) with no path-prefix condition or ABAC restriction. Investigation data is stored at per-investigation paths (s3://bucket/$cluster_id/$investigation_id/$date/$task_id/), but any rosa-boundary task role holder can read or list any object in the bucket, regardless of which SRE owns that investigation.
findings.impact: SRE Alice can connect to her own investigation container and use the task role credentials (available at $AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI inside Fargate) to call `aws s3 ls s3://rosa-boundary-audit/bob-cluster/bob-investigation/ --recursive` and `aws s3 cp s3://rosa-boundary-audit/bob-cluster/bob-investigation/ /tmp/ --recursive`. She retrieves Bob's full investigation artifacts: downloaded cluster data, kubeconfig outputs, must-gather results, and any credentials written to disk. The s3:ListBucket permission makes investigation enumeration trivial even without knowing exact paths. This requires no role assumption or privilege escalation — the task role credentials are available to any process running inside the container.
findings.recommendation:
Scope S3 permissions to the caller's own investigation prefix. Since each investigation has a per-investigation task definition registered by the Lambda with CLUSTER_ID and INVESTIGATION_ID baked in as environment variables, the Lambda can register the task definition with a tightly-scoped resource ARN:

```hcl
Resource = [
  "${aws_s3_bucket.audit.arn}/${cluster_id}/${investigation_id}/*"
]
```

For the read permission specifically, consider removing s3:GetObject from the task role entirely — the entrypoint only needs s3:PutObject for audit sync. If read access is needed, use a separate IAM condition:

```json
{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": ["s3:PutObject"],
  "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::${bucket}/${cluster_id}/${investigation_id}/*"
}
```

findings.title: SSMSessionForECSExec Has No Ownership Tag Condition — Direct SSM Session Path Bypasses ABAC
findings.issue: The SSMSessionForECSExec statement in the shared SRE role grants ssm:StartSession on arn:aws:ecs:*:*:task/* (any ECS task ARN in any region/account) with no tag condition. The policy comment acknowledges this: 'The SSM API does not have access to ECS resource tags.' The ABAC ownership check is exclusively enforced by the ecs:ExecuteCommand IAM action — not by SSM. An SRE holding the shared SRE role credentials can call ssm:StartSession directly with a manually constructed target string (ecs:<cluster>_<taskId>_<containerRuntimeId>) for any ECS task, without the ecs:ExecuteCommand prerequisite being evaluated in the SRE role's policy context. The containerRuntimeId for any task is obtainable via ecs:DescribeTasks, which is unconditionally permitted by DescribeAndListECS.
findings.impact: SRE Alice can bypass the ABAC ownership gate by: (1) calling ecs:DescribeTasks on Bob's task to get the containerRuntimeId; (2) constructing the target string ecs:<cluster>_<bob-taskId>_<runtimeId>; (3) calling aws ssm start-session --target <target> --document-name AWS-StartInteractiveCommand --parameters command=/bin/bash using her sre_shared role credentials. If SSM does not independently verify ecs:ExecuteCommand authorization (this is an AWS-internal enforcement that is not reflected in the IAM policy itself), Alice enters Bob's container. This completely bypasses the two-statement ABAC design because SSM does not evaluate ECS resource tags.

findings.title: KMS Key Policy Grants Decrypt to All Fargate Tasks Without SourceArn Condition
findings.issue: The KMS key policy's 'Allow ECS Exec' statement grants kms:Decrypt and kms:GenerateDataKey to Principal: {Service: ecs-tasks.amazonaws.com} with Resource: '*' and no aws:SourceArn or aws:SourceAccount condition. Combined with the shared SRE role's KMSForECSExec statement (which also grants kms:Decrypt on Resource: '*', see M2), the exec session KMS key can be used by: (a) any Fargate task in the AWS account regardless of workload, via the service principal grant; (b) any authenticated SRE via the SRE role IAM policy grant, allowing direct API calls to kms:Decrypt outside the CloudWatch Logs access path.
findings.impact: Any Fargate task in the account that knows this KMS key ARN can decrypt data encrypted with it. If other ECS workloads share the same AWS account, they can use this key. More practically, any SRE with the sre_shared role can call kms:Decrypt directly with raw ciphertext from CloudWatch Logs, bypassing CloudWatch's own access controls. When M3 is remediated (CloudWatch log encryption enabled), this means SRE Alice can decrypt Bob's SSM session logs (ciphertext retrievable via cloudwatch:GetLogEvents on the shared log group) without Bob's knowledge or any additional IAM permission.

findings.title: close-investigation EFS Access Point Deletion Has No Ownership Verification
findings.issue: The close-investigation command finds the EFS access point using FindAccessPointByTags(ctx, closeClusterID, closeInvestigationID) where both parameters are caller-supplied CLI flags. The lookup filters by ClusterID and InvestigationID EFS tags only — there is no comparison of the access point's oidc_sub or username tag against the caller's identity. Any SRE who knows another user's cluster_id and investigation_id (discoverable via list-tasks, see H3) can delete their EFS access point without any ownership check. This extends H6 with a specific code-level finding in the EFS lookup path.
findings.impact: SRE Alice runs: rosa-boundary close-investigation --cluster-id <bob-cluster-id> --investigation-id <bob-investigation-id> --force --yes. The command: (1) finds Bob's EFS access point by tag match; (2) stops Bob's running task (H6); (3) deregisters Bob's task definition; (4) deletes Bob's EFS access point — permanently destroying access to Bob's investigation home directory. Bob loses all in-progress investigation data not yet synced to S3. The EFS data physically remains on the filesystem but is inaccessible without the access point, and recreating it at the same path would require Lambda re-invocation.

findings.title: ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition Exposes Per-Investigation Environment Variables and Secrets ARNs to All SREs
findings.category: Infrastructure — Information Disclosure via Overly Permissive IAM Policy
findings.issue: The DescribeAndListECS statement grants ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition on Resource: '*' with no conditions. Per-investigation task definitions (registered by the Lambda at handler.py:441-545) contain investigation-specific data baked into environment variables: CLUSTER_ID, INVESTIGATION_ID, OC_VERSION, S3_AUDIT_BUCKET, TASK_TIMEOUT. The kube-proxy sidecar's secrets section contains the Secrets Manager ARN for the cluster kubeconfig: arn:aws:secretsmanager:<region>:<account>:secret:rosa-boundary/clusters/<cluster_id>/kubeconfig. The task definition family name (format: {base_family}-{cluster_id}-{investigation_id}-{timestamp}) is derivable from the task's taskDefinitionArn field, which is visible in ecs:DescribeTasks output.
findings.impact: SRE Alice calls ecs:DescribeTasks on Bob's task, obtains the taskDefinitionArn, then calls ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition on that ARN. She reads Bob's exact CLUSTER_ID, INVESTIGATION_ID, S3_AUDIT_BUCKET path, and the partial ARN of Bob's cluster kubeconfig in Secrets Manager. While Alice still needs secretsmanager:GetSecretValue to read the kubeconfig (not granted by the SRE role), knowing the exact secret ARN is a meaningful stepping stone. The S3 path disclosure compounds C1: Alice now has the exact bucket path for Bob's audit data without having to enumerate. This is a concrete, specific instance of H3 (information disclosure via DescribeAndListECS) focused on the task definition inspection vector.
findings.recommendation:
Remove ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition from the shared SRE role's DescribeAndListECS statement. The join-task and list-tasks workflows do not require task definition inspection. If task definition details are needed for the close-investigation flow, that operation should use the Lambda or a separate admin role with appropriate logging. The ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition permission can be added back with a resource ARN scoped to the project family prefix if absolutely required:

```hcl
Resource = [
  "arn:aws:ecs:${data.aws_region.current.name}:${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:task-definition/${var.project}-${var.stage}*"
]
```

findings.title: Invoker Role sts:TagSession Is Inert — No Invoker Policy Statement Uses Session Tags
findings.issue: The lambda_invoker role trust policy allows sts:TagSession alongside sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity. This causes session tags from the JWT's https://aws.amazon.com/tags claim to be applied to the invoker role session. However, the invoker role's permissions policy (aws_iam_role_policy.lambda_invoker) grants only lambda:InvokeFunction on one specific Lambda ARN — it has no tag-conditioned statements. The sts:TagSession in the trust policy creates no actual security enforcement: no invoker policy checks aws:PrincipalTag/* for any purpose. The session tags on the invoker session are set and then completely unused.
findings.impact: This is a misleading security signal: code reviewers may assume the sts:TagSession on the invoker role provides ABAC enforcement on Lambda invocation, but it does not. If a future developer adds permissions to the invoker role (e.g., ecs:DescribeTasks for debugging), they may incorrectly believe those permissions are tag-restricted because sts:TagSession is present. The real risk is a future false-sense-of-security vulnerability. Additionally, sts:TagSession in a trust policy technically allows the caller to inject arbitrary session tags (up to the STS session tag limits) into the invoker role session — these tags are unused but are recorded in CloudTrail, which could pollute audit logs.
findings.recommendation:
Either remove sts:TagSession from the invoker role trust policy (since it provides no security enforcement) and add a comment explaining why it is absent, or add a comment explaining that the session tags are inert on the invoker role and are only meaningful on the sre_shared role:

```hcl
# Note: sts:TagSession is intentionally NOT included here because the invoker role
# has no tag-conditioned policy statements. Session tags from the JWT are meaningful
# only when assuming the sre_shared role, where they enforce ABAC.
Action = ["sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"]
```

findings.title: join-task Standalone Uses Ambient Credentials — ABAC Enforcement Is Optional, Not Structural
findings.issue: The standalone join-task command loads ambient AWS credentials via config.LoadDefaultConfig() without requiring OIDC authentication or sre_shared role assumption. If an SRE has ambient credentials with ecs:ExecuteCommand permission (e.g., administrator role, developer role, EC2 instance profile, environment variables set by a CI system), join-task will use those credentials directly, bypassing the ABAC ownership check. The ABAC enforcement only applies when the caller's ambient credentials happen to be the sre_shared role — which is the expected case after start-task, but is not structurally enforced. By contrast, start-task always goes through OIDC authentication and explicit role assumption.
findings.impact: An operator or developer with broad AWS permissions who runs rosa-boundary join-task <any-task-id> with administrator credentials can exec into any SRE's investigation container without the ABAC ownership check applying. The ecs:ExecuteCommand call succeeds with whatever IAM permissions the ambient credentials have. This also means that in environments where AWS credentials are set via environment variables for testing or CI, any join-task invocation bypasses the intended ABAC model. The architectural intent (OIDC → ABAC role → exec) can be completely circumvented for exec operations using only the join-task command.

findings.title: Official AWS CLI Downloaded Without Checksum or Signature Verification
findings.issue: The official AWS CLI v2 is downloaded from `awscli.amazonaws.com` via `curl -o /tmp/awscliv2.zip` and immediately unzipped and installed without any checksum or PGP signature verification. AWS publishes SHA256 checksums (`awscliv2.zip.sha256`) and a PGP signature file alongside each release at `https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-<arch>.zip.sig`. Neither is fetched. A compromised CDN endpoint, DNS hijack, or BGP hijack during the build could substitute a malicious archive containing backdoored CLI binaries that subsequently get baked into the image.
findings.impact: The AWS CLI binary runs inside every SRE investigation container with access to the task role IAM credentials. A backdoored `aws` binary could exfiltrate IAM credentials, S3 audit data, ECS task metadata, or establish a reverse shell using the Bedrock or ECS Exec IAM permissions available to the task role. Since the binary is baked into the image at build time, all subsequent investigation sessions would be compromised until a new image is built.
findings.recommendation:
