Download and verify the AWS CLI checksum before installation:

```dockerfile
RUN AWS_CLI_ARCH=$(cat /tmp/aws_cli_arch) && \
    curl -o /tmp/awscliv2.zip "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-${AWS_CLI_ARCH}.zip" && \
    curl -o /tmp/awscliv2.zip.sha256 "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-${AWS_CLI_ARCH}.zip.sha256" && \
    sha256sum -c /tmp/awscliv2.zip.sha256 && \
    unzip -q /tmp/awscliv2.zip -d /tmp && \
    /tmp/aws/install --install-dir /opt/aws-cli-official --bin-dir /usr/local/bin/aws-cli-bin && \
    rm -rf /tmp/awscliv2.zip /tmp/awscliv2.zip.sha256 /tmp/aws
```

For stronger assurance, also verify the PGP signature using AWS's published public key.

findings.title: OpenShift CLI Tarballs Downloaded and Piped to tar Without Integrity Verification
findings.issue: Seven OpenShift CLI versions (4.14–4.20) are downloaded from `mirror.openshift.com` and piped directly to `tar -xzf -` without any checksum verification. The `curl -sL ... | tar -xzf -` pipeline is particularly dangerous because: (1) if `curl` fails mid-stream, tar may decompress a truncated or corrupted file and silently extract a partial binary; (2) `curl -sL` follows redirects (`-L`) without restriction, so a DNS compromise or redirect injection on `mirror.openshift.com` could serve a malicious tarball; (3) Red Hat does publish SHA256 checksums for OC CLI releases (alongside each tarball as `.sha256` files and in `sha256sum.txt` files) but they are not fetched; (4) the shell error code from the pipe is the exit code of `tar`, not `curl`, so a failed download does not abort the build under `set -e`.
findings.impact: A compromised or substituted OC CLI binary is baked into all seven installed versions. The CLI binary runs with the task role IAM credentials and full access to connected ROSA clusters. A backdoored `oc` could exfiltrate kubeconfig credentials, cluster API tokens, or cluster data. Since multiple versions are installed, a single compromised mirror response compromises all sessions regardless of which OC version the SRE selects. The `curl | tar` pipeline also means a build failure mid-download is silent — the binary at `/opt/openshift/${version}/oc` may exist (from a previous layer) but be an older version, creating version confusion.
findings.recommendation:
Download and verify checksums before extraction:

```dockerfile
RUN OC_SUFFIX=$(cat /tmp/oc_suffix) && \
    for version in 4.14 4.15 4.16 4.17 4.18 4.19 4.20; do \
      mkdir -p /opt/openshift/${version} && \
      BASE_URL="https://mirror.openshift.com/pub/openshift-v4/clients/ocp/stable-${version}" && \
      curl -fSL -o /tmp/oc-${version}.tar.gz "${BASE_URL}/openshift-client-linux${OC_SUFFIX}.tar.gz" && \
      curl -fSL -o /tmp/oc-${version}.sha256 "${BASE_URL}/sha256sum.txt" && \
      grep "openshift-client-linux${OC_SUFFIX}.tar.gz" /tmp/oc-${version}.sha256 | sha256sum -c - && \
      tar -xzf /tmp/oc-${version}.tar.gz -C /opt/openshift/${version} oc && \
      chmod +x /opt/openshift/${version}/oc && \
      rm -f /tmp/oc-${version}.tar.gz /tmp/oc-${version}.sha256; \
    done
```

Using `-f` (fail on HTTP error) in curl prevents silent 404/error responses from being piped to tar.

findings.title: Container Image URI Has No Digest Enforcement — Mutable Tag Allows Silent Image Substitution
findings.issue: The `container_image` Terraform variable (line 29–32) has no validation rule requiring a digest (`@sha256:...`) suffix. The ECS task definition at `ecs.tf:96` uses `var.container_image` directly. If an operator specifies a mutable tag (e.g., `123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/rosa-boundary:latest`), then: (1) the per-investigation task definition registered by the Lambda at handler.py:476 inherits this mutable tag by reading `base_td['containerDefinitions']`; (2) if the ECR tag is overwritten (by a new build or a compromised CI pipeline), newly registered investigation task definitions will reference the new image; (3) existing running investigations continue using the originally pulled image, but all new investigations launched after the tag update silently run the new, potentially compromised image. There is no mechanism in the current codebase to detect or alert on tag drift.
findings.impact: A compromised CI/CD pipeline that pushes a malicious image to the ECR repository with the same tag would silently serve the malicious image to all new SRE investigation sessions without any operator notification. Since SRE containers run with Bedrock access, S3 audit bucket write access, and ECS Exec SSM channel permissions, a backdoored image has significant impact. The Lambda-registered task definitions inherit the mutable tag, meaning the compromise propagates to all investigations created after the poisoned push.

findings.title: aws s3 sync Follows Symlinks by Default — Sensitive Files Can Be Exfiltrated via /home/sre
findings.category: Application — Container — Sensitive Data Exfiltration via Symlink
findings.issue: The `sync_to_s3()` function runs `aws s3 sync /home/sre "${S3_AUDIT_ESCROW}" --quiet` at lines 28–29. The AWS CLI `s3 sync` command follows symbolic links by default. An SRE with interactive shell access (via ECS Exec) can create a symlink in `/home/sre` pointing to any file readable by root on the host filesystem — for example `ln -s /etc/passwd /home/sre/exfil-passwd` or `ln -s /proc/1/environ /home/sre/env`. When the container exits (or when a SIGTERM is received), the entrypoint's cleanup function syncs `/home/sre` to the audit S3 bucket, following the symlink and uploading the target file. Since the entrypoint runs as root (PID 1) and S3 sync runs as root, it can follow symlinks to root-readable files. The SRE user also has NOPASSWD sudo (M8), enabling creation of symlinks to any file on the container filesystem.
findings.impact: An SRE can exfiltrate: (1) `/proc/1/environ` — the entrypoint's full environment including any injected secrets, the `ECS_CONTAINER_METADATA_URI_V4` endpoint URL, and any environment variables set by ECS; (2) `/proc/self/net/tcp` — active network connections revealing internal VPC endpoints; (3) container filesystem files such as `/etc/sudoers.d/sre`, `/usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh`; (4) any credential files that happen to be on the root filesystem. The exfiltrated files land in the audit S3 bucket, which the SRE can read back via the task role's `s3:GetObject` permission (see C1 for the read scope issue). This provides a path to read container internals and environment without needing direct root access beyond what sudo already grants.
findings.recommendation:
Pass `--no-follow-symlinks` to prevent symlink traversal:

```bash
aws s3 sync /home/sre "${S3_AUDIT_ESCROW}" --quiet --no-follow-symlinks ||
    echo "Warning: S3 sync failed" >&2
```

Note: `--no-follow-symlinks` causes `s3 sync` to skip symlinks entirely (they are not uploaded as symlinks or as their targets). This is acceptable since legitimate SRE investigation artifacts should be real files. Also consider adding `--exclude '.*'` to prevent syncing hidden dotfiles containing credentials (e.g., `.aws/credentials` if accidentally created).

findings.title: No USER Directive in Containerfile — Default Runtime User Is root
findings.issue: The Containerfile has no `USER` directive. The final image's default user is `root` (UID 0). The entrypoint script runs as root (PID 1) and deliberately does not drop to the `sre` user for the main process — the CLAUDE.md notes that 'ECS Exec sessions connect as the sre user via the CLI's default `runuser -u sre -- bash` command.' However, this architecture means: (1) the container default user is root; (2) if the ECS Exec session is initiated without the `runuser` wrapper (e.g., by calling `ecs:ExecuteCommand` directly with a command other than the default), the session runs as root inside the container; (3) any tooling that inspects the container image's default user (e.g., image scanning policies that reject root-user images) will flag this image; (4) the CMD `sleep infinity` runs as root.
findings.impact: The absence of a USER directive means the image violates OCI best practices and most container security policies (e.g., Kubernetes PodSecurityPolicy `runAsNonRoot`, OpenShift SCCs). While Fargate provides isolation between tasks, an operator mistake in ECS Exec invocation (omitting the `runuser` wrapper) results in a root shell inside the container with full access to modify system files, alter the entrypoint, and access any process or file. This is particularly relevant given that the entrypoint runs as PID 1 as root with signal handling for audit sync — any root shell access can kill PID 1 with SIGKILL (H4).
findings.recommendation:
The entrypoint architecture (root for alternatives, then drops to sre) makes adding a permanent USER directive complex. The recommended approach is to restrict what root can do after the initial setup:

1. Add `USER sre` as the final directive and modify the entrypoint to use `sudo` only for the specific `alternatives --set` commands (requires M8 fix to scope sudo to `/usr/sbin/alternatives`).
2. Alternatively, use a setuid wrapper script for `alternatives` so the entrypoint can be run as the `sre` user from the start.
3. At minimum, document in the ECS task definition or operator runbook that ECS Exec sessions MUST use `runuser -u sre -- bash` and not connect as root.

findings.title: ECS Task Definition Missing noNewPrivileges and Capability Drop
findings.issue: The `rosa-boundary` container's `linuxParameters` block (lines 137–139) sets only `initProcessEnabled = true`. It does not set `noNewPrivileges: true` or drop any Linux capabilities. On AWS Fargate, the default capability set is limited (no `NET_ADMIN`, `SYS_ADMIN`, etc.), but the following capabilities are present by default and could be used by a root-capable SRE: `CHOWN`, `DAC_OVERRIDE`, `FOWNER`, `FSETID`, `KILL`, `SETGID`, `SETUID`, `SETPCAP`, `NET_BIND_SERVICE`, `NET_RAW`, `SYS_CHROOT`, `MKNOD`, `AUDIT_WRITE`. `noNewPrivileges` prevents privilege escalation via setuid binaries; without it, if a setuid binary is inadvertently installed by a DNF package, it could be exploited inside the container. The `kube-proxy` sidecar sets `readonlyRootFilesystem = true` but also lacks `noNewPrivileges`.
findings.impact: Without `noNewPrivileges`, a setuid or setcap binary on the container filesystem could be used by the `sre` user to gain elevated privileges even without explicit sudo access. While the default Fargate capability set is restricted, `NET_RAW` allows raw socket operations (port scanning, packet injection to internal VPC endpoints). `SYS_CHROOT` allows chroot operations that could be used to pivot to alternate filesystem contexts. Dropping unnecessary capabilities reduces the blast radius of any container breakout or insider threat scenario.
findings.recommendation:
Add `noNewPrivileges` and drop all non-essential capabilities in the `linuxParameters` block:

```hcl
linuxParameters = {
  initProcessEnabled = true
  capabilities = {
    drop = ["NET_RAW", "SYS_CHROOT", "MKNOD", "AUDIT_WRITE", "SETPCAP", "FSETID"]
  }
}
```

Note: `noNewPrivileges` is not a standard ECS `linuxParameters` field — it maps to the Docker `--security-opt no-new-privileges` option. In ECS Terraform, this requires the container definition to include a `dockerSecurityOptions` field:

```hcl
dockerSecurityOptions = ["no-new-privileges"]
```

Verify Fargate platform version compatibility before deploying capability drops.

findings.title: skip_task=True Bypasses Duplicate-Investigation Check — Any Authenticated SRE Can Retrieve Another User's EFS Access Point ID
findings.issue:
When the client supplies `skip_task=True` in the request body (line 134), the duplicate-task ownership check at lines 591-599 is entirely skipped (`if not skip_task:`). The function then calls `find_existing_access_point()` (line 586, which always runs) and returns the existing access point ID in the HTTP 200 response body (line 643) if one is found. This means any authenticated SRE can call the Lambda with `skip_task=True` plus a known or guessed `cluster_id` and `investigation_id` to retrieve the EFS access point ID for an investigation owned by a different user. The `skip_task` parameter is accepted from the client with no ownership validation — there is no check that the caller owns the investigation or its associated access point before returning the ID.

The duplicate-task check that does run for `skip_task=False` (lines 592-599) also leaks the access point ID in the 409 error response via `DuplicateInvestigationError.access_point_id`, but that path at least requires a task collision. With `skip_task=True`, the caller gets the access point ID on every call regardless of collision state.
findings.impact: An authenticated SRE can retrieve any other user's EFS access point ID by calling the Lambda with `skip_task=True` and the victim's known `cluster_id`/`investigation_id` (discoverable via H3's `ecs:DescribeTasks`/`ecs:ListTasks`). The access point ID is the primary identifier needed to mount the EFS access point directly (subject to network access), and enables the attacker to construct the full S3 audit path at `s3://bucket/cluster_id/investigation_id/`. Combined with C1 (unrestricted S3 read), the attacker can enumerate and read the victim's complete investigation artifacts. Additionally, `skip_task=True` creates a new EFS access point if one does not exist — effectively squatting on an investigation ID namespace before the legitimate user does.

findings.title: Reaper Deadline Comparison Strips Timezone Without UTC Conversion — Non-UTC Offset Tags Cause Late Reaping
findings.category: Application — Logic Error in Time Comparison
findings.issue:
The reaper parses the deadline tag with `datetime.fromisoformat(deadline_str.replace('Z', '+00:00'))` and then strips timezone info with `deadline.replace(tzinfo=None)` without first converting to UTC. For a deadline string with a non-UTC offset (e.g., `2026-06-01T12:00:00+05:30`), `fromisoformat` correctly parses this as UTC+05:30 (which is 06:30 UTC), but `replace(tzinfo=None)` naively strips the `+05:30` to produce `2026-06-01T12:00:00` — a naive datetime treated as UTC 12:00. The comparison `now > deadline` (where `now = datetime.utcnow()`) then compares against 12:00 UTC instead of 06:30 UTC, causing the reaper to delay stopping the task by 5 hours 30 minutes beyond the actual deadline.

For the normal production code path (deadlines produced by `datetime.utcnow().isoformat()` in create-investigation), the deadline string has no timezone suffix, so `fromisoformat` returns a naive datetime, the `if deadline.tzinfo is not None` branch is never entered, and the comparison is correct. The bug only triggers if: (a) a task's deadline tag is manually set or modified with a non-UTC offset, or (b) a future code change generates timezone-aware deadline strings.
findings.impact: A malicious SRE with access to modify ECS task tags (which the task role does not grant, but the Lambda execution role grants via `ecs:TagResource` on `*`) could set the deadline tag to a future timestamp with a large positive UTC offset, causing the reaper to skip the task for hours beyond the real deadline. At minimum, this is a latent correctness bug: the code comment says 'Remove timezone info for comparison (both are UTC)' which is only true because the current producer (create-investigation) generates naive UTC datetimes. The defensive `replace(tzinfo=None)` is incorrect — it should be `.astimezone(timezone.utc).replace(tzinfo=None)` to correctly handle any offset.
findings.recommendation:
Replace the naive tzinfo strip with a proper UTC conversion:

```python
from datetime import datetime, timezone

# Correct: convert to UTC before stripping tzinfo
deadline = datetime.fromisoformat(deadline_str.replace('Z', '+00:00'))
if deadline.tzinfo is not None:
    deadline = deadline.astimezone(timezone.utc).replace(tzinfo=None)
```

This ensures that regardless of the timezone offset in the deadline string, the comparison is always against a correctly converted UTC time. Also consider using timezone-aware datetimes throughout by replacing `datetime.utcnow()` with `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` to avoid accidental mixing of aware and naive datetimes.

findings.title: create-investigation Lambda Grants ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition and ecs:DeregisterTaskDefinition on Resource='*' With No Cluster Scope
findings.issue: The existing M1 finding identifies the wildcard ECS resource in the create-investigation Lambda policy. This follow-on finding specifically highlights `ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition` and `ecs:DeregisterTaskDefinition` which cannot be scoped to a cluster ARN by AWS IAM (they operate at the account level, not cluster level). The Lambda registers a new task definition on every investigation creation (handler.py:655) with arbitrary container definitions derived from the base task definition. A compromised Lambda could register task definitions with: (1) modified image URIs pointing to attacker-controlled registries; (2) additional containers with backdoors; (3) environment variables containing exfiltrated credentials. These registered task definitions persist in the account and can be launched by any principal with `ecs:RunTask`. `ecs:DeregisterTaskDefinition` can also deregister legitimate task definitions, blocking future investigations.
findings.impact: If the Lambda is compromised (OIDC bypass, dependency supply chain), the attacker can register persistent malicious task definitions in the AWS account that outlive the Lambda compromise. Other ECS clusters or operators with `ecs:RunTask` could inadvertently launch these malicious definitions. The wildcard resource scope for `RegisterTaskDefinition` is an inherent AWS IAM limitation, but the risk can be mitigated by naming conventions and separate monitoring.

findings.title: abac_values[0] Not Validated as String Type Before Use as ECS/EFS Tag Value
findings.issue:
At line 196, `abac_tag_value = abac_values[0]` takes the first element of the `abac_values` list extracted from the JWT's `principal_tags` claim without verifying it is a string. The list element could be any JSON type: a number, boolean, nested list, or dict. While AWS's `principal_tags` claim is expected to contain strings, a misconfigured or malicious Keycloak mapper could produce `{"principal_tags": {"username": [42]}}` or `{"principal_tags": {"username": [{"nested": "object"}]}}`. A non-string value is passed through to ECS `run_task` tags (line 684: `{'key': abac_tag_key, 'value': abac_tag_value}`), where boto3's serializer will either raise a `ParamValidationError` (for complex types) or coerce to string representation (for simple types like `42` → `'42'`). The ABAC condition `ecs:ResourceTag/username == ${aws:PrincipalTag/username}` would then fail to match (STS session tag is the JWT string value; ECS tag would be the boto3 coercion), causing all ECS Exec attempts to be silently denied.

This finding is distinct from M23 which covers the tag value's length and character set. This is about the Python type before any string operations are applied.
findings.impact: A misconfigured Keycloak protocol mapper that produces a non-string value in `principal_tags` causes the ABAC tag to be set to a coerced string (e.g., `'42'`), which will never match the STS session tag (which carries the original JWT value). The SRE can create investigations but can never exec into them — a silent denial of service for that user. Alternatively, a complex type causes a boto3 `ParamValidationError`, orphaning the EFS access point (M12).
findings.recommendation:
Add an explicit type check immediately after extracting `abac_values[0]`:

```python
abac_tag_value = abac_values[0]
if not isinstance(abac_tag_value, str):
    logger.warning(f"ABAC tag value is not a string (type: {type(abac_tag_value).__name__}) for user {username}")
    return response(403, {'error': f'Invalid ABAC claim format: {ABAC_TAG_KEY} value must be a string'})
```
